serving.description: 1 bowl with rice and side dishes
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 700 g, comprising a bowl of beef bone broth with tender beef shank, a portion of white rice, sliced rice cakes (tteok), a soft-boiled or poached egg, sweet radish, and a drizzle of sesame oil, accompanied by traditional Korean side dishes such as kimchi and pickled radish.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — composite estimate derived from: Beef shank, cooked, simmered (FDC ID 174032); Beef broth, ready-to-serve (FDC ID 171532); White rice, cooked (FDC ID 169756); Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled (FDC ID 173424)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore entry exists for Beef Gomtang Gukbap as a composite dish. Per-100g nutrient values were cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central component entries for beef shank (cooked, simmered), beef broth, cooked white rice, and hard-boiled egg. The input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with a mixed beef-broth-and-rice dish at approximately 107 kcal/100g and are accepted as an estimated composite. The original input listed 'grabfood' as data_source, which is a forbidden primary source per Project Veritas v2 source policy; values have been independently cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central component data and are accepted as estimated composites with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. White rice, boiled (GI = 72, glucose reference); University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (boiled, GI 72).
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Beef Gomtang Gukbap as a composite dish. GI estimated at 72 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being white rice (GI 72, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). Tteok/rice cakes have a higher GI (~82-85) but white rice is the primary carbohydrate contributor by weight in this dish. Glycemic load per 700g serving calculated as: (72 × 65.0g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 46.8, rounded to 47.0. Available carbohydrate = 9.29g/100g × 700g = 65.0g. This constitutes a high glycemic load meal.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium load from beef bone broth, salt seasoning, and sodium-rich side dishes (kimchi, pickled radish)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 257 mg/100g yields approximately 1,800 mg per 700g serving before accounting for additional sodium from kimchi and pickled radish side dishes; individuals with hypertension should avoid drinking the full broth, request reduced-salt preparation, and limit or omit high-sodium side dishes to keep total meal sodium below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load from white rice and tteok (rice cakes) with low dietary fibre (0.57 g/100g), producing a high glycemic load of approximately 47 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: The combination of white rice and tteok creates a glycemic load of approximately 47 per standard 700g serving, well above the high-GL threshold of 20; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice, limit or omit tteok, and consume a smaller portion (300-350g) to reduce postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef shank (1.71 g/100g) and trans fat (0.07 g/100g), combined with high sodium and cholesterol (25.71 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 700g serving provides approximately 12g saturated fat, 0.5g trans fat, and 180mg cholesterol alongside approximately 1,800mg sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 300-350g, choose lean cuts over fatty beef shank, avoid drinking the full broth to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to partially offset the atherogenic lipid profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (beef shank) is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acids), which promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production; high glycemic load from white rice and tteok further drives systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef shank with oily fish (salmon, mackerel) or tofu to reduce arachidonic acid intake, limit or omit tteok to reduce glycemic inflammatory load, and retain kimchi as a side dish for its probiotic anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Gomtang Gukbap (소곰탕국밥) is a traditional Korean comfort dish consisting of tender beef shank slow-simmered for several hours in a rich, milky-white beef bone broth (gomtang), served over or alongside a bowl of steamed white rice. The dish is characterised by its deeply savoury, collagen-rich broth with a clean, mild flavour profile, contrasting with the soft, yielding texture of the braised beef shank. In this Singaporean restaurant version, the bowl is further garnished with sliced tteok (Korean rice cakes), a soft-boiled or poached egg, sweet pickled radish, and a drizzle of sesame oil, accompanied by traditional Korean side dishes including kimchi and pickled radish (danmuji). Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 107 kcal per 100g, with a moderate protein content of 7.86g/100g from the beef shank and egg, 4.29g/100g fat (predominantly from beef and sesame oil), and 9.29g/100g carbohydrate primarily from white rice and tteok. Dietary fibre is low at 0.57g/100g. Sodium is elevated at 257mg/100g, reflecting the salt-seasoned broth and fermented side dishes. A standard 700g serving delivers approximately 750 kcal, 55g protein, 30g fat, and 65g carbohydrate with a high glycemic load of approximately 47, making it a high-energy, high-GL meal. Clinically, the dish poses significant concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (high GL, refined carbohydrates), hypertension (high sodium from broth and sides), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium). The collagen-rich bone broth provides glycine and proline, which may support joint health. Gomtang is a staple of Korean home cooking and is widely consumed as a restorative meal, particularly during cold weather or recovery from illness.
